coaching.subject: actual output of the eval runner command

coaching.coaching: State the concrete attestation evidence the [operator-attested] locating reference for the 33 eligible reviews will require (a change id / vote outcome / timestamp), recorded on the ticket.
coaching.subject: locating reference for the 33 eligible reviews

coaching.coaching: Realign child deliverable format versus parent epic requirements to the parent's plan — the parent wins on conflict; if the parent is genuinely wrong, update the PARENT first (which forces its re-review), never silently diverge the leaf.
coaching.subject: child deliverable format versus parent epic requirements

coaching.coaching: Pin down decision rule mapping eval output to verdict with a concrete worked example.
coaching.subject: decision rule mapping eval output to verdict

coaching.subject: verification pathway for each acceptance criterion

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] AC1 names `rebar prompt eval plan-review-novelty` as the runner, but that command is offline-only (spec validation, no model calls, no per-case verdicts); replace or supplement it with the actual live-run invocation (e.g. the eval CI workflow dispatch, a `rebar criteria eval` call, or a direct `run_eval()` script) and confirm it emits per-case discriminates_novelty pass/fail output.
evidence: src/rebar/_cli/_llm_eval_commands.py lines 18-96: `_prompt_eval` outputs {valid, epochs, gate, coverage_threshold, gating_scorers, report_scorers, gold_set_size, dirty_prompt_sha256} — no model calls, no per-case scoring

src/rebar/_cli/_llm_eval_commands.py line 92-95: 'live run needs the `eval` extra + credentials (eval CI); commit the prompt to apply a passing edit.' — the offline command explicitly disclaims live execution
src/rebar/llm/evals/eval.py lines 613-693: `run_eval()` is the live path; it is not reachable from `rebar prompt eval` CLI handler

src/rebar/llm/eval_specs/README.plan-review.md lines 57-62: 'The live model run (the actual recall/false-fire scoring across epochs) needs the `nava-rebar[eval]` extra + model credentials and runs in the eval CI'
EVIDENCE CLASS: Verified (observed) — the CLI handler source is read directly
findings.finding: `rebar prompt eval plan-review-novelty` is an offline spec-validation command that outputs spec metadata (epochs, gate, scorer names, gold-set size, prompt SHA) — it does NOT execute model calls, run the discriminates_novelty scorer, or emit per-case pass/fail verdicts. AC1 requires 'the score/verdict per case' but the named command cannot produce that output; the live model run needs `nava-rebar[eval]` extra + API credentials and a different invocation path (e.g. the eval CI workflow or `run_eval()` directly).
findings.location: AC1: '`rebar prompt eval plan-review-novelty` (or the documented equivalent runner surfaced by `rebar prompt eval --help`)' and 'The comment includes the exact command, the score/verdict per case'
scenarios: Operator runs `rebar prompt eval plan-review-novelty`, gets only spec-metadata output (no per-case verdicts), and cannot satisfy AC1's requirement for 'score/verdict per case' — the AC is silently unachievable with the named command.

The 'documented equivalent runner surfaced by `rebar prompt eval --help`' does not exist as a live-run variant — `--help` only surfaces the offline validation path, leaving the operator without a clear path to the live run needed to diagnose the zero-drop anomaly.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the AC1 command with the live-run path: either (a) `rebar criteria eval plan-review-novelty` if that invocation triggers `run_eval()` for this spec, or (b) document the eval CI workflow dispatch (`workflow_dispatch` on `eval-plan-review-container.yml` or an equivalent) as the attested runner, with its JSON output attached. Also remove the '(or the documented equivalent runner surfaced by `rebar prompt eval --help`)' fallback, since `--help` only shows the offline path.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the capability assertion in AC1: replace `rebar prompt eval plan-review-novelty` with the actual live-run command (e.g. the eval CI invocation or direct `inspect_ai` / eval-CI equivalent) that produces per-case scores, and confirm the `[eval]` extra and credentials are available in the operator's environment.
evidence: src/rebar/_cli/_llm_eval_commands.py lines 17-23: 'Validates the git-tracked eval spec (offline; grader discipline + at_least(k) + coverage) and reports the DIRTY working-tree prompt's content hash. The live model run needs the `eval` extra + credentials (the eval CI)'
src/rebar/_cli/_llm_eval_commands.py lines 67-95: the `_prompt_eval` function emits only spec metadata fields (epochs, gate, gating_scorers, gold_set_size, dirty_prompt_sha256) — no per-case scores
src/rebar/llm/evals/eval.py lines 22-25: 'only the actual run (:func:`run_eval`) imports Inspect AI, lazily, behind `guard_import`' — rebar prompt eval does not call run_eval
AC1 requires 'the score/verdict per case' — this output is only available from a live Inspect AI run, not from the offline validator
findings.finding: `rebar prompt eval plan-review-novelty` is an offline spec-validator, not a live-model runner; it will not produce 'score/verdict per case' as AC1 requires.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC1: '`rebar prompt eval plan-review-novelty` (or the documented equivalent runner surfaced by `rebar prompt eval --help`)'
scenarios: An operator runs `rebar prompt eval plan-review-novelty` per AC1 and gets only offline spec validation output (no per-case model scores), leaving the central question (scorer healthy vs defective) unanswered.
The plan's hedge ('or the documented equivalent runner') is unresolvable via `rebar prompt eval --help` since that help does not surface a live-run command.
findings.suggested_fix: Update AC1 to cite the live eval runner (e.g. the `.github/workflows/eval-plan-review-container.yml`-style invocation or `rebar prompt eval plan-review-novelty --live` if that flag exists) and add a prerequisite note that the `[eval]` extra (`pip install 'nava-rebar[eval]'`) and an API key must be present. Drop the `--help` hedge since `--help` does not surface a live-run command.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State where the 33 eligible reviews are located (e.g., a stored query, a date range filter command, or a prior ticket/report ID) so the executor can identify them without investigation.
evidence: 'post-flip field data shows 33 remediation-eligible reviews produced ZERO novelty drops'
No query, dataset file, or prior ticket reference is given to locate these 33 records
An executor stopping to discover which reviews qualify constitutes a clarification round-trip
findings.finding: The '33 remediation-eligible reviews' are referenced with the definite article as a known set, but the plan provides no locating reference (no query, no file path, no prior ticket ID, no date-range filter) that would let an executor identify which 33 reviews to examine.
findings.location: AC2: 'The 33 eligible-but-zero-drop reviews are re-examined against the eval outcome'
scenarios: Executor runs the eval but cannot identify which 33 reviews to examine for the AC2 conclusion, requiring them to investigate or guess the dataset.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a locating note such as: 'The 33 eligible reviews are the remediation-eligible reviews logged since the flip date; retrieve them with `rebar reviews list --status remediation-eligible --after <flip-date>` or by referencing the dataset at <path/ticket>.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the divergence between the leaf's investigative-spike scope (no code change) and the parent epic's AC requiring every child to land a Gerrit change at +1: either amend the parent AC to explicitly exempt investigative spikes from the Gerrit-vote requirement, or add a follow-up code-change scope to this leaf so it has a Gerrit-landable deliverable.
evidence: Parent AC: 'Every child's change landed on main via Gerrit with both gate votes at +1 (visible in the change's vote history at rebar.solutions.navateam.com)'
Parent Success Criteria: 'The six child tickets below are closed with their changes landed on main through Gerrit'
Leaf file_impact: [{"path": "src/rebar/llm/reviewers/plan_review_novelty.md", "reason": "read-only: eval target, no edit planned"}]
Leaf verify_commands: [] (empty — no Gerrit change, no CI gate)
findings.finding: The leaf is a pure investigative spike (all deliverables are ticket comments; file_impact is read-only; no verify_commands) but the parent epic's Acceptance Criteria explicitly require every child's change to land on main via Gerrit with LLM-Review +1 AND Verified +1. The leaf cannot satisfy that parent AC, making it diverge from the parent's completion contract.
findings.location: Parent AC: 'Every child's change landed on main via Gerrit with both gate votes at +1' vs leaf file_impact 'read-only: eval target, no edit planned' and leaf verify_commands: []
scenarios: When this ticket is closed with only ticket comments and a conditional bug filed, the parent's 'all six children landed on main via Gerrit' AC cannot be attestably satisfied, stalling the parent epic's own plan-review.
If the scorer is healthy and no code change is warranted, the leaf has no path to producing a +1-gated Gerrit change — the parent AC becomes permanently unsatisfiable for this child.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a clause to the parent epic's AC such as: 'Investigative-spike children (no code change produced) satisfy this AC by closing with operator-attested ticket comments; the Gerrit +1 requirement applies only to children with code changes.' Alternatively, amend this leaf's AC3 so that if the scorer is defective, fixing it (not just filing a bug) is in scope here, producing a Gerrit-landable change.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a co-located decision rule to AC2 specifying which eval outcome patterns (e.g., widespread `nov is None` → schema mismatch; all cases fail at 0.5 midpoint → discrimination failure; all pass at midpoint but runtime drops still zero → check T_novel config) map to 'defective scorer' vs 'healthy scorer, drops correctly withheld'.
evidence: Plan text: 'silently failing scorer (the fail-safe returns zero drops on error)'
decide.py:89-91: `if not scores: return 0.0` — runtime fail-safe returns 0.0 (carryover = never dropped, regardless of scorer health)
eval_scorers.py:374-375: `if nov is None: return ScoreResult(True, False, 'no novelty/matches_prior to discriminate on')` — eval fail-safe returns a FAIL verdict, not zero drops

eval spec plan-review-novelty.eval.yaml line 24: 'distinct from the runtime drop threshold T_novel=0.7 (cc5b)' — the eval discriminator (0.5 midpoint) is not the same as the runtime threshold the plan's hypothesis references

AC2 requires a one-paragraph conclusion stating whether 'the scorer discriminates (and drops were correctly withheld) or the scorer is defective' — but no protocol maps a specific eval outcome pattern to each diagnosis
findings.finding: The plan's conclusion-writing step (AC2) must distinguish 'scorer is defective' from 'healthy precision-first quadrant', but the plan provides no co-located decision rule tying the eval output (per-case pass/fail at the 0.5 midpoint) to the runtime drop behavior (T_novel=0.7 threshold). The two fail-safe paths the plan alludes to — runtime `decide.novelty()` returning 0.0 (carryover, never dropped) vs. the eval scorer returning `ScoreResult(passed=False)` when the output lacks a `novelty` field — have different semantics, yet the plan collapses them under 'the fail-safe returns zero drops on error' without specifying which path corresponds to 'defective scorer' vs 'healthy scorer that never fired'.
scenarios: An operator sees the eval pass (all 6 cases score correctly at the 0.5 midpoint) but runtime still produces zero drops because T_novel=0.7 > midpoint — they conclude 'healthy' but the runtime threshold asymmetry means the eval passing does not rule out misconfiguration of T_novel.

An operator sees the eval fail on some cases (nov is None → ScoreResult passed=False) and concludes 'defective scorer', when actually the LLM output format diverged from the expected schema, not the scorer logic itself.

evidence: 2 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit healthy-case oracle (e.g., `rebar deps <this-id> | grep discovered_from || echo 'no bug filed — healthy verdict confirmed'`) so the end-state is verifiable by command in both branches.
evidence: The oracle sentence is conditional on the defective case only
The healthy-case outcome ('this ticket's conclusion says so') has no corresponding proving command
An executor cannot mechanically verify the healthy-case outcome beyond reading prose
findings.finding: AC3's oracle (`rebar deps <this-id>`) only specifies the proving command for the defective-scorer case; the healthy-scorer case has no stated verification command, leaving 'this ticket's conclusion says so' as an unanchored prose claim with no deterministic check.
findings.location: AC3: 'if healthy, this ticket's conclusion says so and no bug is filed. Oracle: rebar deps <this-id> shows the discovered_from link in the defective case.'
scenarios: Executor concludes the scorer is healthy, writes the conclusion, and closes the ticket — but there is no specified command to confirm the absence of a bug link, risking an incomplete or incorrectly closed ticket.
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC3 oracle to: 'Oracle (defective): `rebar deps <this-id>` shows the discovered_from link. Oracle (healthy): `rebar deps <this-id>` returns no discovered_from link and the conclusion comment is present on the ticket.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define 'sidecar example' in the plan body (or AC2 itself) — specify what artifact it refers to, where it is stored, and how to locate the ticket ids for the 33 eligible-but-zero-drop reviews.
evidence: AC2 states: 'cites at least two concrete sidecar examples by ticket id'
The plan body never defines 'sidecar', never describes where sidecar data lives, and never explains how the 33 reviews are associated with ticket ids that carry sidecar artifacts
findings.finding: The term 'sidecar examples' in AC2 is undefined in the plan body, leaving it ambiguous what an implementer should look for when citing 'at least two concrete sidecar examples by ticket id'.
scenarios: Implementer runs the eval, writes the conclusion paragraph, but cannot identify what counts as a 'sidecar example' or where to find ticket ids for the 33 reviews, leaving AC2 unverifiable.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence to the body such as: 'Sidecar examples are the per-review JSON artifacts stored alongside each plan-review ticket (e.g. at <path>); each artifact carries the originating ticket id used for citation.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a locating reference for the '33 remediation-eligible reviews / zero novelty drops' baseline: cite the sidecar corpus query, script, or prior ticket that produces this count so it can be reproduced and the AC2 'two concrete sidecar examples' can be grounded.
evidence: No file, query, ticket, or script in the repo yields the '33 eligible reviews' dataset or the 'zero drops' count.
Search for '33 eligible', '33 remediation-eligible reviews', 'zero drops', 'remediation-eligible reviews' returns no codebase hits.
The plan uses the definite article ('the 33 remediation-eligible reviews') implying a known, locatable corpus, but no pointer to it is given.
findings.finding: The plan's central premise — '33 remediation-eligible reviews produced ZERO novelty drops' — is stated as established fact but has no locating reference (no file path, no prior ticket ID, no script, no query) anywhere in the codebase. This is an empirically-checkable field-data claim that cannot be verified by Grep/Read, and an unverifiable assertion is a gap under E4 fail-closed rules.
findings.location: Plan body: 'post-flip field data shows 33 remediation-eligible reviews produced ZERO novelty drops'
scenarios: The implementer cannot reproduce or challenge the '33 reviews / zero drops' baseline without knowing where it comes from.

If the field data came from an informal count or a now-stale sidecar corpus, the AC2 conclusion ('cite at least two concrete sidecar examples by ticket id') cannot be satisfied without independently re-discovering the set.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Acknowledge in the plan body that the rising floor is already unconditionally active (novelty_drop_active retired in 4cdf, operator-authorized 2026-07-11), so a defective scorer finding in the eval means drops may already be occurring in field reviews, not merely a future activation risk.
evidence: src/rebar/_config_schema.py:274-277: 'The rising floor is always active (shared with the code-review region-gated floor, ADR 0037; operator-authorized on field evidence, 2026-07-11, in lieu of 150b's discriminates_novelty eval). It still runs subject to remediation eligibility + per-review self-gates; the off switch (novelty_drop_active) was retired in story 4cdf.'

src/rebar/llm/plan_review/__init__.py:180-182: 'The rising floor is always active (operator-authorized on field evidence, 2026-07-11 — in lieu of 150b's discriminates_novelty eval; the off switch was retired in story 4cdf)'
docs/adr/0008-convergent-plan-edit-re-review.md:58-61: 'both flags now default ON — operator-authorized on field evidence in lieu of the discriminates_novelty eval'.
findings.finding: The plan frames the eval as an open evidence gate — 'This task determines which [hypothesis], with evidence' — but the codebase records that the rising floor was already activated unconditionally: 'operator-authorized on field evidence, 2026-07-11 — in lieu of 150b's discriminates_novelty eval; the off switch was retired in story 4cdf' (src/rebar/_config_schema.py:275, src/rebar/llm/plan_review/__init__.py:181). The plan's implicit premise that the eval outcome would govern whether the floor is active is contradicted by the code.
findings.location: Plan body: 'This task determines which [scorer discriminates vs. defective scorer], with evidence, and files the follow-up' / AC3: 'if healthy, this ticket's conclusion says so and no bug is filed'
scenarios: If the eval passes (scorer is healthy), the plan says 'no bug is filed' and concludes the zero drops were correct. But since the floor is already always-active independent of the eval, a 'healthy' verdict has no actionable consequence for the floor's activation state.

If the eval fails (scorer is defective), the plan prescribes filing a bug — but since the floor is already running unconditionally, a defective scorer would mean the floor is dropping findings without a valid novelty signal, which is a more urgent issue than the plan implies.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Augment the diagnostic approach: in addition to running the synthetic eval, inspect the actual novelty scores and drop_reason fields from the 33 production sidecar records to confirm whether {} was returned (fail-safe/exception path), scores were sub-0.7, or priorities were >= 0.4 — so the conclusion is grounded in production data, not only synthetic-eval proxy results.
evidence: Plan states two hypotheses: 'healthy precision-first quadrant (novel findings rarely both score >= 0.7 novelty AND < 0.4 priority)' vs 'silently failing scorer (the fail-safe returns zero drops on error)'
The eval spec uses 3 synthetic pairs tested at 0.5 midpoint, not the 33 production cases
docs/plan-review-gate.md: 'A failed/malformed novelty sub-call defaults novelty to 0.0 (carryover → never dropped)'
src/rebar/llm/plan_review/__init__.py:163: except Exception: return {} — fail-safe returns empty dict, yielding no drops

ABSENCE: The plan never includes a step to inspect the actual novelty scores from the 33 production sidecars, which would directly confirm whether {} was returned (fail-safe) vs sub-threshold scores vs priority >= floor
findings.finding: The plan's diagnostic mechanism (running the synthetic discriminates_novelty eval) is a proxy that cannot fully distinguish the two stated hypotheses. The eval tests scorer discrimination on 3 toy pairs at a 0.5 midpoint, but the runtime drop condition requires novelty >= T_novel=0.7 AND priority < 0.4. A scorer that passes the eval could still produce zero production drops because: (a) the fail-safe exception path fired in the 33 real reviews (returning {} with no scores, hence no drops), (b) all real findings scored in [0.5, 0.7), or (c) all novel findings had priority >= 0.4. Since the sidecar records for those 33 reviews should contain the actual per-finding novelty scores and drop_reason, inspecting them directly would give ground-truth evidence that the synthetic eval cannot provide.
findings.location: Plan body — 'This task determines which, with evidence' / Acceptance Criteria AC2
scenarios: Eval passes (scorer discriminates on toy data), implementer concludes 'healthy', but the real 33 reviews all hit the exception path and returned {} — the scorer was actually silently failing in production, and the eval result was a false negative on the defective hypothesis.

Eval passes, but all 33 production novelty scores were in [0.5, 0.7) — below T_novel — and the plan's conclusion attributes this to 'precision-first quadrant' when it was actually a threshold calibration issue.
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC or body step: 'Before drawing the conclusion, examine a sample of the 33 production REVIEW_RESULT sidecars (e.g. via `.rebar/cache/plan-review/<ticket-id>/`) for the `novelty` field per finding and any `drop_reason`; if all novelty values are 0.0 this confirms the fail-safe path fired in production regardless of the eval result.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a one-sentence rationale in the plan body explaining why the synthetic eval is the right (or sufficient) diagnostic for the production zero-drop observation — or explicitly state it is a first gate and production sidecar inspection is the confirming step.
evidence: Plan says: 'Run the standing discriminates_novelty eval against the plan-review novelty scorer... and record the result' — no rationale given for why this is sufficient
ABSENCE: No explanation of why the eval is preferred over or sufficient compared to inspecting production sidecar records
findings.finding: The plan states no positive rationale for using the synthetic standing eval rather than directly inspecting production sidecar data. The only implicit justification is that the eval is 'standing' — but this doesn't address why it is the right diagnostic tool for a question that is primarily about production behavior (33 real reviews, zero real drops), not scorer isolation.
scenarios: Implementer follows the plan, gets an eval pass, and writes the conclusion without knowing whether the eval actually answers the production question — because the plan never explains the connection between synthetic-eval discrimination and production drop behavior.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
